chunk_summary: Adolescent vegetarian nutrition counseling focuses on protein distribution, anemia screening, and addressing dietary risks like ultra-processed foods and restrictive eating patterns, with tailored interventions for athletes and girls with heavy menstrual bleeding.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional risks associated with adolescent vegetarian diets, including anemia and protein deficiency.
Learn strategies for protein distribution across meals to support growth and activity levels.
Identify and address dietary patterns like breakfast skipping, reliance on ultra-processed foods, and restrictive dieting.
Develop culturally appropriate interventions for athletes and adolescents with micronutrient deficiencies.
key_facts: Adolescence is a high-risk period for vegetarian diet failure due to increased autonomy, growth demands, and menstrual iron losses in girls.
Girls with heavy menstrual bleeding and vegetarian diets may require hemoglobin testing and iron therapy.
Protein distribution should include two daytime anchors: breakfast (e.g., milk, sprouts, tofu) and post-school or post-training snacks.
Overweight adolescents may still face iron or B12 deficiencies due to low-quality diets; interventions should focus on reducing refined carbohydrates while preserving protein and diversity.
Athletes should consume protein-containing snacks soon after training using culturally acceptable foods rather than expensive powders.
reference_quote: Position of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics: Vegetarian Diets (2016) — 'Well-planned vegetarian diets are appropriate for individuals during all stages of the life cycle, including adolescence.'
